mnemonics.title: Label Operations
mnemonics.content:
PUSH = Put Label ON TOP (entering MPLS)
SWAP = Switch Label (in transit)
POP = Pull OFF Label (leaving MPLS)

mnemonics.title: MPLS Header Fields
mnemonics.content:
L - Label (20 bits) = LABEL
E - EXP (3 bits) = EXPERIENCE/QoS
B - BoS (1 bit) = BOTTOM
T - TTL (8 bits) = TIME

LEBT: Label EXP BoS TTL - Remember as "Label BoS is at BOTTOM, TTL runs at TOP"

mnemonics.title: LSR Functions
mnemonics.content:
LER = Label Edge Router (Edge - Entrance/Exit)
LSR = Label Switched Router (Core - Switch)

Edge = PUSH and POP (Classification)
Core = SWAP only (Fast forwarding)

mnemonics.title: LIB vs LFIB
mnemonics.content:
LIB = Label Information Base (ALL bindings - Control Plane)
LFIB = Label Forwarding Information Base (ACTIVE forwarding - Data Plane)

LIB: I = Incomplete/Inventory
LFIB: F = Forwarding/Functional

acronyms.usage: The router device in MPLS that performs label-based forwarding
Edge router that performs label imposition and removal (same as Edge LSR)
The unidirectional logical path through MPLS network established by labels
Group of packets that are forwarded identically through MPLS network
Protocol for distributing label bindings between LSRs
Control plane table containing all learned label bindings
Data plane table used for actual packet forwarding based on labels
Optimization where penultimate router removes label before sending to egress
Bit indicating whether current label is the last in the stack
Technology that enables label-based packet forwarding in networks

visualTips.visualization: Draw a packet as a stack: IP Header → Shim Header (Label) → Data Link. Show PUSH adding new label on top, SWAP replacing top label, POP removing top label.
Draw network diagram: [IP Network] → [Ingress LER] → [Core LSR] → [Core LSR] → [Egress LER] → [IP Network]. Show arrows indicating PUSH at ingress, SWAP in core, POP at egress.
Two boxes side by side: LIB box contains all labels (lots of entries), LFIB box contains only best labels (fewer entries). Arrow from LIB → LFIB showing selection process.
32-bit box divided: [20-bit Label][3-bit EXP][1-bit BoS][8-bit TTL]. Label at left, BoS at position 22, TTL at right. BoS=1 means bottom of stack.

analogies.analogy: IP routing is like reading a detailed map at every intersection (longest prefix match). Label switching is like having a ticket with a seat number - just show it and be directed to your destination (exact match lookup).
FEC is like airline boarding classes - all passengers in Business class (same FEC) get the same treatment, same routes, same priority, regardless of their specific destination within that class.

The label stack is like nested envelopes. Each router opens (Pops) the outer envelope, reads the instructions (Label), and passes it to the next router. PHP is like the second-to-last person just handing over the inner envelope without opening the outer one.

An LSP is like a dedicated express train track. All passengers (packets) on this track follow the same path, stop at the same stations (LSRs), and don't need to figure out the route themselves - they just follow the label path.

LIB is like a complete phonebook with all numbers you ever learned. LFIB is like your speed-dial contacts - only the important ones you actually use for calling. The speed-dial is a subset optimized for quick access.
